title: Your own Skills and slash commands
subtitle: Project Skills in .claude/skills: describe a technique once — the whole team uses it forever

theory.text: A familiar power — now in the terminal

theory.md:
You already know Skills from World 3: a folder with a `SKILL.md` that captures a ready-made method — how to do a specific task the right way. In Claude Code, Skills work exactly the same way, but here they truly come into their own: an agent in the terminal doesn't just read instructions, it carries them out — running scripts, editing files, running checks.

The big news of this lesson: Skills can be **project-level**. They live right in the repository, in the `.claude/skills/` folder, get committed to Git along with the code — and are automatically available to everyone working on that project. Describe once how your team ships a release or writes a migration, and that technique is known to every developer's agent.

rows: Only you, across all your projects
Personal techniques: your commit style, your report templates
The whole team — the folder is committed to Git
Rules and procedures of a specific project: release, migrations, deploy
Ready-made Skills from marketplaces — more on that in World 6

theory.md:
The difference between the personal and project levels is the difference between "my habit" and "our standard." A skill "format PR descriptions with this template" in `~/.claude/skills/` helps only you. The same skill in the project's `.claude/skills/` lands in the repository, reaches your colleagues with their next `git pull` — and every one of their agents starts formatting PRs the same way. No mass emails saying "team, we have a new policy": the policy just lives in the code.

If a personal and a project skill have the same name, the more specific one wins — the project one: project rules outrank personal habits.

theory.text: How a skill is built: nothing new

theory.title: .claude/skills/release-notes/SKILL.md
theory.code:
---
name: release-notes
description: Compiles release notes from commits since the last tag. Use when asked to prepare release notes, a changelog, or a release description.
---

# Release notes

1. Collect commits since the last tag: git log <tag>..HEAD --oneline
2. Group them: New / Fixes / Other
3. Write in English, without commit hashes
4. Save the result to docs/releases/<version>.md

theory.md:
The anatomy is the same as in World 3: a folder named after the skill, containing a `SKILL.md` with frontmatter (`name` and `description`) and instructions. Alongside it you can put helper files — templates, scripts, examples: the agent pulls them in when the skill fires.

A skill fires in two ways. **Automatically** — Claude reads the `description` and decides on its own that the skill fits the task: you ask "prepare release notes" and the agent finds the skill by its description and applies it. And **explicitly, via a slash command**: type `/` and the skill's name — `/release-notes` — and it runs immediately, no guessing. Every skill is at the same time a slash command: `/skill-name` appears in the suggestions after you press `/`, right next to built-in commands like `/help` and `/init`.

theory.bad:
---
name: helper
description: A useful skill for various project tasks.
---
theory.good:
---
name: db-migration
description: Creates a database migration following the project's rules. Use when asked to add or change a table, column, or index.
---
theory.explanation: Auto-invocation of a skill depends entirely on the description: Claude picks a skill by matching its description against the task. "A useful skill for various tasks" matches no specific request — such a skill will never fire. A good description names what the skill does and in which phrasings to call it. The rule from World 3 holds here too: the description decides everything.

theory.text: How a skill differs from CLAUDE.md

theory.md:
It may look like Skills duplicate CLAUDE.md — both hold instructions. The difference is the economics of context. CLAUDE.md loads **in full, always**, into every session — which is why it must stay short: only what's needed constantly. A skill loads **in full only when needed**: in an ordinary session only a short description of it is present, while the full instructions and files are pulled in the moment it fires.

Hence the rule for choosing: everyday rules (run commands, code conventions, forbidden zones) go in CLAUDE.md; occasional multi-step procedures (release, migration, report) go into Skills. Ten pages of release policy in CLAUDE.md would eat memory in every session; in a skill they cost nothing until needed.

The Claude Code docs (the Skills page) describe Skills as reusable sets of instructions in folders with a SKILL.md: personal ones in `~/.claude/skills`, project ones in the repository's `.claude/skills`. Claude applies a skill automatically when its description matches the task, and any skill can be invoked explicitly by a slash command with its name.

The best candidate for your first project skill is a procedure you've already explained to the agent in chat twice. Once you notice you're dictating the same steps again, say: "Turn these steps into a project skill called release-notes." Claude will create the folder in .claude/skills and write the SKILL.md itself — all that's left is to review and commit it.

tasks.instruction: You're in a Claude Code session in the shop-api project. A colleague says: "We have a release-notes skill, just call it." Invoke the skill explicitly — with a slash command by its name.

steps.output:
● Applying skill release-notes (.claude/skills/release-notes/SKILL.md)

  Collecting commits since the last tag v2.3.0...
  Grouping into sections...

● Done: docs/releases/v2.4.0.md
  • New: QR-code payment, filter orders by date
  • Fixes: duplicate emails on repeat orders

  All per the policy in SKILL.md — same as the whole team.
steps.failHint: Invoking a skill explicitly is a slash command with its name: /release-notes

tasks.successMessage: Exactly: every skill is at the same time a slash command. /skill-name runs it immediately, with no reliance on auto-firing.

tasks.question: Where must a skill live so the whole project team gets it automatically?
options: In CLAUDE.md — paste the whole skill text in
On the company's shared network drive
tasks.explanation: The .claude/skills/ folder lives in the repository and is committed to Git — the skill reaches colleagues along with the code, and everyone's agent works by the same rules. The personal folder ~/.claude/skills/ is visible only to you, and pasting into CLAUDE.md would bloat every session's memory.
tasks.hint: Whatever gets committed to Git is what's shared.

tasks.question: A two-page multi-step release procedure. Where should it go and why?
options: In CLAUDE.md — it loads automatically, after all
In a skill: the full instructions load only when needed, not every session
Nowhere — dictate it to the agent from scratch each time
In README.md — the agent will figure out to read it
tasks.explanation: That's the key difference from CLAUDE.md: project memory loads in full, always, so it's reserved for short everyday rules. A skill, by contrast, "sleeps," taking up space only with a short description, and unfolds in full only when it fires. Long, occasional procedures are skill territory.
tasks.hint: Recall the economics of context: what loads always, and what loads on demand.

tasks.instruction: Here's the SKILL.md of a project skill. One line guarantees it will never fire automatically. Find it.
lines: description: A skill that does various useful checks.
2. Check that .env didn't get into the commit
3. Make sure the version in package.json is bumped
tasks.explanation: Auto-invocation of a skill is decided by the description: Claude matches the description against the current task. "Various useful checks" matches no real request — the agent will never realize the skill is needed. A working version: "Verifies readiness to deploy: tests, secrets, version. Use before a deploy or when asked to check a release." The skill's steps themselves are written well.

tasks.instruction:
A mission in real Claude Code. **You need Claude Code installed — if you don't have it, come back to this mission later.**

1. Open a terminal in any of your own projects and run `claude`.
2. Ask: "Create a project skill commit-style: commits are written in English, the first line is up to 60 characters, and it ends with what exactly was verified." Check that a `.claude/skills/commit-style/` folder with a SKILL.md appeared.
3. Open the SKILL.md and make sure the description is specific: it says what the skill does and when to apply it. If it's vague — fix it.
4. Type `/` and find your skill in the list. Invoke `/commit-style` and watch the agent apply your rules.
checklist: The .claude/skills/commit-style/ folder with a SKILL.md was created
The description is specific: what it does and when to apply it
The skill shows up in the slash-command list after pressing /
